---
title: "HIV Molecular Network Analysis & Priority Cluster Evaluation (San Diego)"
---

## Overview

This project evaluates how well CDC-defined HIV molecular clusters identify ongoing transmission.

Using simulated HIV transmission and genetic network data calibrated to the San Diego epidemic (2019–2021), I analyzed how molecular clusters are detected over time and assessed whether these clusters capture individuals contributing to transmission.

---

## Data & Study Design

- **Study design:** Simulation-based modeling study using agent-based transmission network framework (SD-ABM-FAV), calibrated to observed molecular HIV network data  
- **Data source:** Simulated HIV transmission and genetic network data  
- **Study period:** 2019–2021 (simulation), calibrated using pre-2018 observed data  
- **Calibration approach:** Model parameters calibrated against empirical molecular network structure and observed transmission patterns  

- **Available data:**
  - Pairwise genetic linkages based on sequence similarity  
  - Timing of sequence collection  
  - Reconstructed transmission network (1,356 transmission pairs)  
  
---

## Statistical Methods

- Constructed molecular networks using genetic distance thresholds (baseline: 0.5%)  
- Performed **threshold calibration (~0.00635)** to align simulated linkages with observed data  
- Compared observed vs simulated networks using:
  - number of genetic linkages  
  - cluster (connected component) size distributions  

- Identified **CDC priority clusters** using:
  - ≥5 diagnoses within 12 months  
  - within a 3-year rolling window  

- Linked molecular clusters to the transmission network to evaluate:
  - whether clustered individuals contributed to forward transmission  

---

## Key Findings

- Simulated molecular networks closely reproduced observed patterns in:
  - number of genetic linkages over time  
  - distribution of cluster sizes  

- Most clusters were small:
  - ~50% consisted of 2 individuals  
  - maximum cluster size was 18  

- CDC-defined priority clusters were rare in both observed and simulated data  

- **No forward transmissions were identified from individuals in priority clusters**  
  → indicating that detected clusters did not capture individuals driving transmission  

---

## Analytical Decisions & Challenges

- **Threshold calibration:** required aligning simulated and observed network structures  
- **Network validation:** used multiple metrics (link counts and cluster size distributions)  
- **Cluster definition sensitivity:** results depended on CDC criteria and time windows  
- **Post hoc limitation:** transmission dynamics could not be altered by simulated interventions  

---

## Impact

- Demonstrates a framework for validating simulated molecular networks against real-world data  
- Highlights limitations of CDC priority cluster definitions in identifying transmission risk  
- Suggests that molecular clustering alone may not adequately capture individuals contributing to HIV spread  
- Supports more comprehensive approaches to HIV surveillance and intervention planning  

## Original Plan vs Actual Analysis

### Original Plan
- Evaluate potential impact of identifying and intervening on HIV transmission clusters  
- Simulate CDC response and measure downstream intervention effects  
- Estimate reduction in undiagnosed time and transmission-related outcomes  

### Actual Analysis
- Focus shifted to evaluating whether molecular clusters accurately capture transmission dynamics  
- Compared observed vs simulated molecular networks  
- Assessed CDC priority clusters in relation to actual transmission network  
- Evaluated whether clustered individuals contributed to forward transmission  

### Key Insight
This analysis suggests that molecular cluster detection alone may not reliably identify individuals driving HIV transmission, highlighting limitations in using cluster-based metrics as direct proxies for intervention targeting.
